data.title: Kinder Morgan: A Top Income Pick For 2026
data.description:
Kinder Morgan (KMI) reported higher-than-expected Q1’26 earnings, driven by double-digit earnings growth in the natural gas business. Kinder Morgan is in an advantageous position as one of North America’s leading energy transporters that connects producer areas such as the Permian to consumer end-markets, and as such is set to benefit from rising natural gas demand. Further, the midstream platform has a $10B investment backlog, which is set to boost the firm's earnings and distributable growth prospects.

In the most recent quarter, Kinder Morgan grew its EBITDA by 18% year-over-year to a record $2.5B, boosted by the pipeline operator’s sizable natural gas operations. I like Kinder Morgan for its large size, potential for earnings-accretive pipeline acquisitions, and dividend growth. Shares also trade below my fair value estimate of $34, meaning investors not only collect a 4% yield, but also have capital appreciation potential.

Previous rating

Kinder Morgan was a strong buy for me back in January 2026 -- Strong Growth, Natural Gas Focus, AI Catalyst -- mainly because of the firm’s exposure to the AI Data Center industry that is hungry for enormous amounts of electricity. With AI adoption accelerating across industries, the need for GPU compute, and therefore energy sources such as natural gas, is a major driver for the operator’s earnings and distributable cash flow growth in the future. With Kinder Morgan having a massive backlog, I believe the midstream enterprise remains a bet on both dividend and earnings growth in 2026.

Kinder Morgan’s natural gas business is on fire

The midstream energy enterprise beat expectations on both the top and the bottom line on April 22, 2026, amid robust demand for natural gas, primarily Kinder Morgan’s largest business segment: KMI published adjusted EPS of $0.48, beating the analyst consensus figure of $0.39 per share easily. KMI's revenues rose ~14% year-over-year to $4.83B, coming in at $281M above the consensus figure.

Kinder Morgan is one of the largest midstream platforms in the North American energy market, as it transports approximately 40% of America's natural gas from producers to end markets. The company owns an interest in or operates ~82,000 miles of pipelines. This includes a natural gas pipeline network that encompasses 65,000 miles and is complemented by more than 700 billion cubic feet (BCF) of working gas storage capacity (~15% of all natural gas storage in the U.S.).

KMI has an operational presence in major production hubs, such as the Permian, Bakken, Utica, and Haynesville Basins (among others), and it is therefore one of the most geographically diversified midstream platforms in the U.S. energy sector.

Kinder Morgan's natural gas business is what drove the firm's outperformance in Q1'26. In the most recent quarter, natural gas pipelines earned Kinder Morgan $1.7B on an EBDA level (essentially EBITDA without the recognition of interest expenses), showing ~18% year-over-year growth. Since natural gas is by far Kinder Morgan's largest business, rising natural gas demand is the single biggest growth catalyst for the pipeline operator. The long-term outlook for U.S. natural gas consumption is positive: according to KMI's internal projections, natural gas demand is set to grow to 150 billion cubic feet per day (Bcf/d) by 2031, highlighting a 27% increase compared to current levels. Since natural gas is the biggest segment for KMI, representing 68% of EBDA, the segment will remain key to Kinder Morgan's growth going forward.

A strong fee structure supports the value proposition for income investors

The most important factor for me to even consider Kinder Morgan as an income investment relates to the midstream platform’s high (96%) percentage of cash flows that are linked to some kind of fee-based contract (i.e., take-or-pay). Take-or-pay contracts, which represent the majority of fee-based contracts (65%) for Kinder Morgan, obligate a customer to pay for a specific capacity or volume even if they don't use the underlying commodity.

Fee-based contracts offer investors the opportunity to take advantage of pre-determined energy prices for the transport of energy raw materials such as natural gas, crude oil, or petrochemicals, which means midstream companies tend to have a lot of cash flow (and dividend) visibility as a result.

Dividend coverage trend

Kinder Morgan has stopped reporting DCF per-share figures in 2024 to align its reporting with standard equity measures in the midstream industry, such as adjusted EPS and free cash flow. However, the midstream enterprise in its supplemental quarterly schedules retroactively discloses its quarterly DCF per-share figure, which is still a useful measure to calculate a midstream platform's dividend coverage ratio, mainly because it strips away non-cash accounting distortions, such as depreciation.

For the most recent quarter, Kinder Morgan did not disclose its quarterly DCF per-share yet, but this figure can be approximated by making certain adjustments to adjusted EBITDA.

I derive Kinder Morgan's per-share DCF by subtracting ~$430M in interest expenses, $220M in maintenance capital (which reconciles to KMI's budget for 2026), and $95M in cash taxes from Kinder Morgan's Q1'26 adjusted EBITDA of $2,539M. Dividing this resulting figure of $1,794M by 2.2B outstanding shares yields an estimated DCF figure of $0.81 per share. This figure in turn implies a dividend coverage ratio of 2.72X for the first fiscal quarter.

I highlight Kinder Morgan's trend in DCF as well as in the coverage ratio in the table below.

KMI Q1'25 Q2'25 Q3'25 Q4'25 Q1'26 (est)
Distributable Cash Flow $0.66 $0.52 $0.56 $0.68 $0.81
Declared Dividends $0.2925 $0.2925 $0.2925 $0.2925 $0.2975
Coverage 2.26 X 1.78 X 1.91 X 2.32 X 2.72 X

Source: Author

For the current fiscal year, Kinder Morgan targets approximately $2.52 per share in distributable cash (according to its latest budget plan), implying 4% year-over-year growth. Since the midstream platform raised its dividend by 2% at the beginning of the year to $0.2975 per share quarterly, Kinder Morgan is on track to publish a forward full-year dividend coverage ratio of 2.12X vs. 2.07X in FY 2025. From a dividend coverage perspective, therefore, Kinder Morgan pays a very well-supported 4% dividend yield that appears to be highly sustainable.

Kinder Morgan’s valuation

The pipeline operator’s shares are valued at an enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of about 11.9X, which places KMI about mid-range in the industry group for large-cap North American midstream companies. Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Enbridge (ENB) and Williams Companies (WMB), three major rivals in the natural gas-focused midstream business, trade at EV-to-EBITDA ratios of 11.0X, 13.3X, and 14.4X, with the industry group average being 12.7X.

If Kinder Morgan revalues to the industry group average EBITDA multiplier, shares of the midstream platform continue to have upside potential in 2026, especially if the company can expand its pipeline footprint on growing natural gas demand. A 12.7X average EV-to-EBITDA ratio implies 7.1% revaluation potential as well as a fair value of $34 per-share for Kinder Morgan.

Primary risks for income investors

From a dividend coverage point of view, Kinder Morgan should have no problems supporting its dividend with distributable cash flow in 2026. The natural gas outlook is also solid, and so is the firm's natural gas segment performance. Nonetheless, there are risks that long-term investors have to factor into their analysis before buying shares of KMI. Specifically, there are three risks that could negatively impact my view on the pipeline operator going forward: 1) Slowing natural gas demand, potentially driven by a recession in the U.S., 2) Moderating growth in the buildout pace of U.S. Data Centers which is a key catalyst for natural gas-driven EBITDA and backlog growth; and 3) A drop in KMI's dividend coverage ratio below 2.0X.

Final thoughts

Natural gas activity continues to remain a significant earnings driver for Kinder Morgan in the first fiscal quarter. Most importantly, growing natural gas demand, in part driven by a growing U.S.-based Data Center footprint, translates into attractive earnings prospects for investors seeking recurring dividend income from an investment grade-rated midstream platform that is tilted towards natural gas deliveries. With Kinder Morgan transporting about 40% of all natural gas in the U.S., the pipeline operator remains a top bet on natural gas expansion and new pipeline acquisitions connecting the company’s facilities to vital production assets located in the Permian and other Basins. Since shares continue to trade below the industry group average, as highlighted above, a strong buy rating is still well-deserved, in my opinion.

data.title: The Trade Desk: Potential Value Trap, Pending FQ1 2026 And Competitive Clarity
data.description:
I previously downgraded The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD) to a Hold in December 2025, attributed to the growth risks from the intensifying ad-tech competition.

In this article, I shall discuss why TTD remains a Hold, pending further clarity on their performance metrics in the upcoming FQ1'26 earnings call.

TTD Faces Numerous Headwinds Entering FY2026

For now, TTD has indeed lost -43.7% of its value while underperforming the wider market at +3.3% and many of its ad-tech peers in varying degrees.

The steep correction has lent credence to my prior downgraded rating, as TTD enters a period of decelerating growth profile, attributed to the intensifying ad-tech competition from numerous Big Tech companies.

1. FQ1'26 Preview

With TTD set to report their FQ1'26 earnings call on May 7, 2026, I urge readers to compare their performance metrics against the last offered guidance at:

  • the revenues of at least $678M (-19.9% QoQ/+10% YoY),
  • the adj. EBITDA of $195M (-51.2% QoQ/-6.2% YoY), and
  • the adj. EBITDA margins of 28.7% (-18.5 points QoQ/-5 YoY/-3.6 from FY2019 levels of 32.3%).

While the management expects to deliver "full year 2026 adjusted EBITDA margin percentage to be approximately in line with 2025" at 41.1% (-0.2 points YoY), I am of the opinion that their mixed FQ1'26 guidance has yet to inspire confidence.

This is especially since TTD has been reporting a decelerating revenue growth profile since 2022, with a similar trend also observed:

  • in FQ4'25 at +14% YoY against a year ago at +22% YoY, and
  • in FY2025 at +18% YoY against a year ago at +26% YoY.

These numbers naturally pale in comparison to its ad-tech peers' outperformance, including:

  • AppLovin (APP), with advertising revenue growth of +70% YoY in FY2025 against a year ago at +75% YoY,
  • Amazon (AMZN) at +22% YoY/+19.8% YoY,
  • Meta (META) at +22.1% YoY/+21.7% YoY, and
  • Google (GOOG) at +11.3% YoY/+11.2% YoY, respectively,

which perhaps explain why TTD's stock prices have underperformed its peers, despite the lifting market sentiments after the double bottoming observed in late March/early April 2026.

2. Changing Advertising Environment

Part of the headwinds may be attributed to two large advertising agencies, Dentsu and WPP plc, reportedly "pulling back from one of The Trade Desk’s most closely-watched, and arguably important, initiatives: OpenPath."

This is supposedly attributed to "concerns about economics and opacity. And because bigger DSP margins compound into higher customer-acquisition costs and weaker ROAS," where TTD supposedly "earns revenue on both sides of the transaction."

This perhaps explains TTD's rather elevated, estimated take rate of 21.5% in FY2025 (+0.8 points YoY), based on the revenues of $2.89B (+18.4% YoY) and the gross advertising spend of $13.39B (+11.2% YoY).

This is compared to AMZN's highly competitive rates at "between 4% and 8%. In some cases, those fees drop as low as 1%—and occasionally disappear altogether," with it underscoring the tech giant's willingness " to undercut the market to win share, especially when it comes to pulling dollars into its DSP."

At the same time, readers may want to note that OpenPath is supposedly TTD's answer to GOOG's Open Bidding, with the former "providing advertisers with direct access to premium digital advertising inventory that helps minimize waste for advertisers while maximizing revenue for participating publishers."

Therefore, while it is uncertain how accurate the market report may be on Dentsu/WPP's supposed withdrawal from TTD's OpenPath platform, it appears that the market has decided to de-risk first, as observed in TTD's underperforming stock prices.

Interested investors may want to tune in to the upcoming earnings call and hear out the management's commentary surrounding OpenPath's adoption trends.

3. CTV Prospects

TTD recently highlighted CTV being "a strong driver of overall growth and remains one of our fastest-growing channels," with it comprising approximately 50% of its business in FQ4'25 compared to "high-40s percentage share" in FQ4'24.

This development is partly attributed to their exposure to numerous publishers and the growing popularity of sports streaming.

TTD has also signaled their plans to expand beyond the US, since its current revenues are still concentrated domestically at 85% in FY2025 (-2 points YoY).

If anything, the international segment has already driven accelerated growth prospects by +34.7% YoY in FY2025 against a year ago at +24.9% YoY, against the decelerating growth observed in the US at +15.9% YoY in FY2025 against a year ago at +26% YoY.

Given the nascency of the international segment, I urge readers to monitor TTD's upcoming FQ1'26 execution and the management's FQ2'26 guidance, since it remains to be seen when their growth re-acceleration may occur.

My caution is attributed to the notably smaller "open web programmatic ad spending" prospects at $40.78B in FY2026 (+6.3% YoY), against walled gardens (such as those within AMZN, META, and GOOG) at $162.2B (+14.2% YoY), with it presenting TTD's growth headwinds against the more aggressive Big Tech peer.

4. Growth Headwinds Already Priced In

Given the decelerating growth trends and the mixed FQ1'26 guidance, it is unsurprising that the consensus has already downgraded their forward estimates, with TTD expected to report a decelerating top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +11%/+14.5% through FY2028.

This is compared to its historical growth profile at a CAGR of +27.9%/+29.9% between FY2019 and FY2025, respectively.

Combined with the -75.2% correction from the 52-week high, TTD has been discounted to FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 7.60x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.26x.

This is down from the recent highs of 37.10x/49.59x and the 5Y mean of 45.14x/61.11x, albeit nearer to the sector median of 7.49x/13.93x, respectively.

When comparing TTD's DSP prospects at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 7.60x and adj. EBITDA margins of 41.1% in FY2025 against its fellow DSP/SSP peers, including:

  • DSP/SSP and hyperscaler/commerce - AMZN at 13.46x/20.3% in FY2025,
  • SSP - Magnite (MGNI) at 7.20x/32.5% in FY2025,
  • DSP/SSP and hyperscaler - GOOG at 18.39x/37.2% in FY2025,
  • DSP/SSP and social media - META at 11.87x/50.6% in FY2025, and
  • DSP/SSP - APP at 22.79x/82.2% in FY2025, respectively,

it may first appear that TTD remains attractively valued here, assuming that they are able to deliver their preliminary FY2026 profit margin guidance.

This development implies that the ongoing 2025/Q1'26 meltdown may have been overdone, albeit with it triggering TTD's potential, deep value play opportunity for the contrarian.

TTD Remains A Hold, Pending Further Clarity

For now, given TTD's ongoing meltdown and the breach against numerous support levels, it is uncertain when a trading floor may materialize.

TTD has also been oversold for some time, signaling a lack of bullish support, worsened by the potential volatility from the elevated short interest ratio of +15.6% at the time of writing.

Based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.26x (near the all-time P/E lows of 9.75x) and the FY2025 adj EPS performance of $1.77 (+6.6% YoY), the stock appears to be trading near to my base-case fair value estimates of $19.90.

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj. EPS estimates of $2.65, there appears to be an excellent upside potential of +27.7% to my base-case, long-term price target of $29.80 as well.

On the other hand, pending further clarity on the FQ1'26 performance and the FQ2'26 guidance, I am of the opinion that TTD may present a potential value trap thesis here, as similarly observed in the management's underwhelming FQ1'26 guidance.

This is worsened by the uncertainty from the ongoing 'executive departures' and 'audit issues,' with it remaining to be seen how things may develop in the near-term.

Combined with the decelerating growth profile, the lumpy profit margin performance, and the oversold technical indicators/discounted valuations implying its value trap investment thesis, I believe that it may be more prudent to wait on the sidelines and observe their near-term performance instead.

TTD remains a Hold here.

data.title: Alibaba's AI Engine Is Powering A Still Undervalued Comeback
data.description:
In early December 2025, I wrote my last article about Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) and as the stock has already declined 20% from its previous high, I argued that investors could take advantage of the recent setback. In the conclusion of my article I wrote:

In my opinion, Alibaba remains one of the better investments for the long run. The company is investing heavily in AI and it is a very diversified business that might profit in several ways from the AI-revolution. And while Alibaba might be able to grow at a high pace (high single digits or even double digits) for several years, the stock is still trading for a more reasonable valuation multiple than several U.S. AI companies.

Of course, this is not the best argument to make, but we should not ignore that Alibaba is still trading about 50% below its all-time high – indicating at least that sentiment was once a lot more bullish. And I see the previous all-time high certainly as a reasonable target within the next one to three years as Alibaba should be able to grow the stock price at a high pace and certainly outperform the market.

However, the stock continued to decline further and dropped below $120 in early April, which resulted in a 37% decline from its previous high. In the last few days, the stock jumped – in line with many other stocks around the world, raising the question whether Alibaba found its bottom.

In the following article, I will argue once again that Alibaba is continuing to trade below its intrinsic value and remains a good long-term investment in my opinion. We will look at the company’s core business (the consumption business), which will also contribute to growth, but not as much as the cloud and AI business, which is currently responsible for a huge part of growth. Finally, we are looking at T-Head, the semiconductor business of Alibaba.

Quarterly Results

We start by looking at third quarter results, which were reported on March 19, 2026 and were rather disappointing. Revenue increased from RMB 280,154 million in Q3/25 to RMB 284,843 million in Q3/26 – resulting in 1.7% year-over-year growth. While the top line still increased a little bit, income from operations dropped steeply from RMB 41,205 million in the same quarter last year to RMB 10,645 million in this quarter – resulting in a 74.2% year-over-year decline. Finally, diluted earnings per share declined 71.0% year-over-year from RMB 2.55 in the same quarter last year to RMB 0.74 in this quarter.

However, we must look at the numbers in more detail as the picture is not as bad as it looks at first glance. Similar to many other AI companies, Alibaba increased its expenditures in the last few years and especially sales and marketing expenses jumped from RMB 42.7 billion in the same quarter last year to RMB 72.0 billion in this quarter – primarily due to the investments in user experiences of the Alibaba China E-commerce Group. We must also point out that Alibaba sold Intime in December 2024 and Sun Art in January 2025, which still had a negative impact on the income statement in the third quarter. When excluding these effects, total revenue increased 9% year-over-year.

Growth Potential

When talking about Alibaba’s growth potential in the years to come, we can focus on three pillars of growth and we start with the core business of Alibaba, which is sometimes overlooked these days as everybody is focusing on cloud and AI. The core business of Alibaba is still its consumption business – mostly the e-commerce business in China, but also its international business.

Consumption Businesses

Alibaba China E-commerce Group, the core business of Alibaba, increased its revenue from RMB 150,589 million in Q3/25 to RMB 159,347 million in Q3/26 – resulting in 5.8% year-over-year growth. But while revenue still increased in the mid-single digits, adjusted EBITDA declined 43% year-over-year to RMB 34,613 million.

And this segment is still struggling a little bit as the China retail sales are still growing, but at a rather slow pace. In March 2026, Chinese retail sales increased 1.7%, which is still a solid growth, but compared to previous growth rates in China, this is rather disappointing.

Especially the quick commerce business is growing with a high pace and increased 56% year-over-year to RMB 20,828 million in this quarter. The Taobao app also saw its monthly active customers increase in the double-digits year-over-year and the 88VIP members, who spend the most on the platform, is still growing in the double-digits and surpassed 59 million.

On the other hand, revenue from customer management, which is responsible for the biggest part of sales, is struggling a little bit. Revenue still increased 0.8% year-over-year to RMB 102,664 million due to weaker transaction activity and the phase-out of the impact of software service fee implementation.

When talking about the retail business (or consumption business), we should not only look at China, but also at the Alibaba International Digital Commerce Group, the international business. In the last quarter it also increased its revenue 3.8% year-over-year to RMB 39,201 million. Adjusted EBITDA for the segment was negative once again, but the picture improved. Instead of a negative amount of RMB 4,952 million in the same quarter last year it was only RMB 2,016 million in this quarter.

Cloud and AI

Growth, however, was mostly driven by the Cloud Intelligence Group, which increased revenue from RMB 31,742 million in Q3/25 to RMB 43,284 million in Q3/26 – resulting in 36.4% year-over-year growth. Adjusted EBITDA also increased 24.6% year-over-year from RMB 3,138 million to RMB 3,911 million.

Management is expecting that growth will stem from three different sources in the years to come. The first will be the MaaS business, Model-as-a-service. Alibaba is expecting that this business will be supported by a variety of different use cases (including the company’s own applications as well as a diverse array of AI application scenarios). Alibaba could generate revenue on a per token basis.

A second driver of growth might be the demand for enterprise-level internal inference and training, which would be like a new marketplace. And management is optimistic this will be an important and long-lasting market:

And that market will continue to exist in the long term. It's not one that will disappear simply because each enterprise makes decisions based on its own business model and the security requirements of its specific use case or the particularity of an application scenario. So for some application scenarios, enterprises will opt to use public MaaS API services, while many others will be based on privately deployed solutions within the enterprise.

Finally, Alibaba is also expecting the “traditional” cloud business to continue growing in the years to come. The CPU-centric cloud computing still has significant room for expansion in this AI-enabled era. The shift towards AI agents might also generate growing demand. During the last earnings call, management commented:

However, in the future, there could be billions of agents that are created by large AI models and their operating environment. The operating environment of these agents will also require substantial support from traditional CPU-centric cloud computing. They need these traditional CPUs as well as databases, storage and large amounts of memory to support their long-term problem solving and sustained operations.

And management is especially optimistic for its combined cloud and AI business and made the following statement during the last earnings call:

Given the enormous and sustained growth momentum of the AI market, combined with Alibaba's full stack positioning across the AI value chain, the business goal of Alibaba's AI strategy is very clear. Over the next 5 years, our goal is to surpass USD 100 billion in combined cloud and AI external revenue, including MaaS.

Looking at the results reported in the last few quarters, Alibaba can certainly be optimistic as revenue for AI-related products was growing in the triple-digits year-over-year for the tenth consecutive quarters.

T-Head

There is a third pillar that might contribute to growth – Alibaba’s semiconductor business T-Head, which was founded in 2018 as a wholly owned subsidiary. T-Head has now shipped about 470,000 AI chips in total so far and the company is reaching an annual revenue level around RMB 10 billion.

Alibaba is not really selling its chips, but it is rather using them internally for its own computing power. But these chips are contributing to the cloud and AI results. During the last earnings call, management was pointing out that over 60% of T-Head chips are being used by external commercial customers – including companies from multiple industries like internet finance, autonomous driving and intelligent manufacturing.

And while acknowledging that T-Head is lagging Western chip makers like NVIDIA, management is seeing the business playing an important role in the next few years and underlined how it is a huge part in Alibaba’s strategy – especially for supply chain resilience. During the last earnings call, management commented:

Because I believe that over the next 3 to 5 years, global AI computing power will be an extremely short supply, especially in the Chinese market, as the only cloud compute company in the Chinese market with proprietary chip development capabilities. T-Head is of paramount importance, therefore, to the Alibaba Group, increasing the supply of AI computing power will help our cloud and AI businesses, including our MaaS business to achieve stronger growth momentum.

It was also reported in January 2026 that Alibaba is planning an IPO for its T-Head subsidiary, but it is unclear when this might happen. In a first step the unit might be restructured as a business partly owned by employees. When asked by analysts during the earnings call, management was not really commenting on more detailed plans about the IPO. It just stated that an IPO is not out of the picture, but a clear timeline does not exist.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

In a final step, we must determine an intrinsic value for Alibaba once again and in a first step we could look at the two simple valuation multiples – the price-earnings ratio as well as the price-free-cash-flow ratio. Both metrics were kind of volatile in the last few months and we should therefore question whether we can draw conclusions from the simple valuation multiples.

At the time of writing, Alibaba was trading for 26 times free cash flow and earnings per share. This is not really indicating that Alibaba is a bargain at this point, but instead of using the simple valuation multiples, I will rather use a discount cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value.

As always, I am calculating with a discount rate of 10% as this is the annual return I like to achieve by an investment annually. Additionally, we are calculating with the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (19,310 million) and as we are calculating with the ADR, we use 2,414 million (exchange rate of 8:1).

Now we could use the free cash flow of the last four quarters as a basis, which was $12,980 million. But we should point out that Alibaba was investing heavily in the last few years (similar to many other technology companies) and the free cash flow of the last four quarters might not necessarily be representative. In the last four quarters, Alibaba reported a free cash flow margin of only 9.2% - clearly below the long-term average of 34.4% (since the IPO). And while we can make the argument that margins were constantly declining in the last few years, which is not a good sign, the current free cash flow margin seems unreasonably low. And similar to many other technology companies, Alibaba has extremely high capital expenditures right now.

Instead, we should be a little more optimistic and assume at least $20 billion in free cash flow as basis here. Additionally, we are calculating with 5% growth for the next ten years (a rather cautious assumption considering the Chinese GDP growth is also expected in the low-to-mid single digits) followed by 4% growth till perpetuity. This leads to an intrinsic value of $147.53 for Alibaba and the stock is more or less fairly valued.

When looking at analysts’ estimates for the years to come, we should calculate with much higher rates. Analysts assume growth rates at least in the high single digits (or maybe even double digits). Let’s calculate with 8% growth in a second calculation followed by 4% growth till perpetuity. This leads to an intrinsic value of $180.12 for Alibaba and would make the stock undervalued at this point.

Finally, I like to raise two more points here. First, a free cash flow of $20 billion still seems like a moderate assumption as Alibaba should have the potential to report a higher free cash flow margin in the years to come.

Second, we could also make the argument for a higher growth rate in 10 years from now. Not only is Alibaba a high-quality business, but the Chinese economy will also grow with a higher pace. When the economy is already growing 4% annually, we can assume that Alibaba might be able to grow 5% or 6% over the long run. This is also making an intrinsic value of $200 to $250 possible.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Alibaba remains undervalued and also is a good long-term investment at this point. The setback in the last few months generated a good buying opportunity.

Despite the challenges, Alibaba is facing – including still rather low retail sales and China probably being affected by the oil shortage and the blocked Strait of Hormuz, which might put the economy under pressure – I remain optimistic about the long-term potential of the business. Especially the company’s cloud and AI business might lead to higher growth rates over several years.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Equity LifeStyle Properties: A Structural Compounder Trading At A Discount
data.description:
Introduction

Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) owns and operates a portfolio of manufactured home (“MH”) communities, recreational vehicle (“RV”) resorts, campgrounds and marinas in North America. While I have written about other residential REITs previously, most recently on UMH Properties (UMH) last month, this will be my first time covering Equity LifeStyle Properties. The company recently released its Q1 2026 earnings, so I figure now would be a good time to evaluate the company.

Business Model

As of 31 March 2026, Equity LifeStyle Properties owns or holds an interest in 453 properties in across 35 states and British Columbia, comprising around 173,400 total sites. Its portfolio spans MH communities (75,700 sites), RV resorts (34,600 annual, 9,800 seasonal, 20,500 transient sites), marina slips (6,900 sites) and membership campground sites (26,000 sites).

The company’s business model is built around land ownership. The company owns the land and leases the developed sites to customers either on a long-term or short-term basis, which creates predictable annual revenue streams. Approximately 91% of revenue comes from stable, annual sources, insulating the company from economic volatility.

Q1 2026 Performance

Equity LifeStyle Properties reported Normalized Funds From Operations (FFO) of $0.84/share, in line with the midpoint of prior guidance of $0.81/share to $0.87/share. Total revenues rose by 2.7% compared to the same period last year, from $387.3 million to $397.6 million. The MH segment, which is also the company’s biggest segment, was the standout performer with base rental income increasing by 5.7% to $195.1 million. This increase was largely drive by an increase in the monthly base average rent per site from $895 to $948, a 5.9% increase. Overall, the company saw a 3.7% increase in property operating revenues.

On the cost side, core property operating expenses rose by just 1.8%, reflecting a positive spread to revenue growth. Additionally, the company completed its property and casualty insurance renewal in April with a premium decrease of approximately 18% while maintaining the same coverage. The savings from this should contribute positively to 2026 margins.

However, there was some weakness as well. Core RV and marina base rental income saw a 1.4% decrease, largely driven by sharp drops in seasonal (-14.8%) and transient (-6.9%) revenue. As a result, management revised its full year 2026 guidance for this segment downwards, though it maintained full year 2026 guidance for Normalized FFO at between $3.12/share and $3.22/share.

Growing Demand… And Limited Supply

Perhaps the most significant advantage Equity LifeStyle Properties has is that it caters to a growing demographic, while at the same time having limited competition.

Over 70% of the company’s MH properties are either age-qualified or have a resident base with an average over 55. Moreover, over 50% of the company’s MH residents are aged 70 or older. Each day, around 10,000 Americans turn 65, and the projected population of people aged 55+ is expected to grow by 14% from 2025 to 2040, ensuring that demand for the company’s properties remain strong and durable. Moreover, the value proposition of purchasing a MH is clear, with a new MH costing 75% less compared to a new single-family home ($125,117 vs $505,867).

It is on the supply front where the company has its biggest competitive advantage. There has been a limited number of new MH developments over the past 20 years, driven by restrictive zoning and regulations. This reduces competition for the company, making replicating its portfolio almost impossible.

With a limited supply and an ever-growing demand, it is no surprise that the company’s occupancy has consistently remained high despite increasing rental prices. While the monthly base average rent per site for Q1 2026 saw a 5.9% increase compared to the same period last year, the company’s occupancy rate remained high at 93.8%, with 50% of communities at 98%+ occupancy.

A Quality Balance Sheet

Equity LifeStyle Properties has a strong balance sheet with low leverage. As of 31 March 2026, the company’s total debt stands at $3.3 billion, giving it a debt to EBITDA ratio of 4.5x. Its maturity schedule is well-laddered, with an average maturity of 8 years, and a weighted average interest rate of 4.1%. Just 6% of its debt is due by 2027, giving the company a long runway and eliminating any near-term refinancing risk. Additionally, 97% of its debt is fixed rate, insulating the company from interest rate volatility and ensuring predictability in cash flows.

In terms of liquidity, the company has around $1.2 billion of capital available to its from its combined line of credit and ATM programs. This is more than sufficient for the company to fund its expansions and acquisitions to continue growing the company.

Reliable and Growing Dividend

When it comes to dividends, the company has an exceptional track record. The company currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.5425/share. Annualized, this equates to $2.17/share and a forward dividend yield of approximately 3.43%. This represents a 5.3% increase over the 2025 dividend, extending the company’s streak to 22 consecutive years of dividend growth. Its 10-year dividend CAGR of 10.6% is also more than double the 4.3% average in the REIT industry.

As mentioned above, the company currently projects Normalized FFO to come between $3.12/share and $3.22/share. Even at the lower end of guidance, this gives the company a dividend payout ratio of 69.55%, ensuring that its dividend sustainability is not in question and also opening the door to further increases.

Key Risks

The clearest near-term risk to the company lies in its RV portfolio. For Q1 2026, the RV portfolio saw a decline in seasonal and transient revenue, leading the company to lower its full-year guidance for this segment. If this weakness persists, it would impact the company’s earnings. However, there are a couple of mitigating factors. First, the RV portfolio represents a smaller portion of the company’s earnings (22.5%) and its impact will not be overly detrimental if the MH segment continues to outperform. Additionally, the annual revenue for the segment actually increased during the quarter (4.2%), which leads me to believe that the current weakness is more seasonal instead of structural.

Another risk is that of interest rates. While the company has done well to lock in the bulk of its debt at a fixed interest rate, a rise in interest rates will still negatively impact the company, especially if it needs to touch on its floating-rate line of credit facility.

Trading At A Premium or Fairly Valued?

Equity LifeStyle Properties is currently trading at approximately 20x forward Normalized FFO. This is significantly higher than the sector median, and at first glance it would seem as if the company is overvalued.

However, the company is trading at a premium for several reasons. The company has consistently outperformed its peers, with its 8.2% Normalized FFO CAGR from 2006 to 2025 almost double that of the 4.2% average for its peers in the residential REIT industry. The company has also shown remarkable stability through various economic cycles. Since 2001, the company has delivered positive NOI growth in virtually every year.

This sentiment is clearly shared by analysts. The average analyst price target for the company is $70.47/share, representing an 11% upside. The consensus rating for the company is also a “Buy”. If anything, it seems to me that analysts view the company as undervalued.

Conclusion

Equity LifeStyle Properties is a high-quality, recession-resistant business which has consistently outperformed its peers. While its dividend is relatively low at 3.43%, the company has been growing its dividends for 22 years, and its comfortable dividend payout ratio indicates this streak will continue for some time to come. At first glance, the company may seem to be overvalued, but there are multiple reasons why it is trading at a premium compared to its peers. For all these reasons, I rate the company as a “Buy” with a price target of $70/share.

data.title: BDCs' Path Hinges On This
data.description:
It is not a secret that the market has priced in a lot of adverse development for BDCs. A big part of the current discounts has been formed by various reasons that have emerged this year - the SaaSacalopyse, private BDC redemption caps and increased scrutiny around marks.

In the meantime, the Q4 2025 data points indicate no systemic struggles. PIK exposures are below average, non-accruals are stable, Q4 earnings commentary embody optimism etc.

So, there is a huge disconnect between what is priced in and what is happening on the ground.

Moreover, if we put the discounts into a broader perspective, we will see that for the current valuations to become justified, there has to be a much greater shock than in the COVID-19, when the non-accruals peaked at 5.2% (in Q2 2020). Currently, the market has assigned a ~12% discount to BDCs (on an asset level). Meanwhile, Q4 2025 BDC non-accruals stand at 3.5%.

Now, it could very well be so that "this time things will be different" and something much worse than what we experienced in the COVID-19 period when the entire economy was shut down could happen.

I'm not buying that.

However, it does not mean that we don't have to pay an attention to this. After all, the market is pretty efficient.

With this in mind, I would like to share how I will be monitoring the situation. If I don't see any signs of deterioration there, I will most probably increase my BDC portfolio exposure even further (form already an overweight position).

Here it comes.

Indicators that will set the course for BDCs

There are a couple of obvious variables that could indicate how BDC loan book quality is evolving and what could be the likely path going forward.

These would be non-accruals and material value markdowns.

However, even if these metrics come in at a solid shape just as pretty much across the board in Q4 2025 reports, the bears could make the following arguments (not exhaustive list):

  • Non-accruals will emerge only when the loan maturity dates kick in. Since most loans come due much later than in Q1 or even in 2026, the non-accrual statistic might not be the best indicator of the underlying situation.
  • Loan investment value markdowns are subjective and just as for non-accruals, the moment of truth will come when the maturity dates kick in.

And I have to say that you can't argue much against that.

So, for this reason I have gone the extra mile by developing a framework to separate noise from the singal, catch it (signal) as soon as possible and, importantly, have a more robust basis for countering the bear arguments.

Let's first level set our common understanding.

I think that we can agree that the most important driver for the current BDC discounts is the potential credit losses. And the two likely sources of these losses would be related to PIK (payment-in-kind) and software company loans.

So, this is what I have done:

1) Selected 5 BDCs with above-average (20%+) portfolio exposures to SaaS or software companies: Hercules Capital, Inc. (HTGC), Sixth Street Specialty Lending, Inc. (TSLX), Fidus Investment (FDUS), Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL) and Golub Capital BDC (GBDC).

2) Based on the most recent 10-k filing (and for Golub Capital it is 10-1Q) compiled number of portfolio companies and their corresponding loans, which mature this year.

3) Determined how many of these loans embody PIK component and how many of them are associated with software sector.

4) Estimated the % of total portfolio income that stems from these loans (it is a rough calculus, where I've multiplied portfolio average yield with the total outstanding principal of these loans, and then divided the result with the full year 2025 total income).

This is what the statistics show:

Metric HTGC TSLX FDUS MSDL GBDC
# of unique companies 12 11 12 15 9
# of loans maturing in 2026 13 14 17 17 23
% of total income 11% 14% 5% 17% 3%
# of software loans 7 7 9 7 7
# of PIK loans 6 5 2 3 2
10-K reference links* Here Here Here Here Here

P.S., for GBDC the most recent data points covering Q4 2025 calendar period are captured by 10-Q report.

There are two very important takeaways from this table that we have to appreciate.

The first takeaway is that the statistics clearly validate that most BDCs have well-laddered portfolio loan investment maturity dates, meaning that a relatively small chunk of the total portfolio becomes subject to full repayments (or refinancings) in the next 12-month period.

It is a great risk management.

Yet the consequence of this is that it all also validates the potential bear arguments, which I highlighted above — i.e., the moment of truth where we will see how high (or low) quality BDC portfolios is unlikely to take place soon, not even this year (at least to a meaningful extent).

Namely, to spot likely catalysts that could trigger further repricing and confirm the validity of current discounts, investors have to stick to the "same old" and traditional indicators: e.g., rising non-accruals, increasing PIK and deep loan investment markdowns.

Having said that, we don't need, say, half of the loan book maturing right now to spot the first signs. Instead, we can keep a very close eye on those ~10 portfolio companies (a rough average from my table), which will have to pay down their loans this year. More importantly, I would pay an extra attention to software and/or PIK related portfolio companies and their loans that have 2026 maturities.

For example, if we see a pronounced pattern of refinancings instead of full repayments or, even worse, maturity amendments coupled with PIK additions, then it would definitely provide us with a reason to pre-emptively trim down select BDC exposures (i.e., those with above average risk). Of course, if these maturing loans don't get repaid or amended, but directly converted to non-accruals, then it would be even more bearish sign.

Finally, it is worth noting that not all these loans have maturity dates in Q1 2026. However, in practice (just as in the public debt markets), companies tend to refinance their loans sooner, say, 3 to 12 months, in order to, among other things, avoid potential risk of hitting a wall with the existing lender and not having sufficient time to making other maneuvers. Simply put, all 2026 loan maturities are worth monitoring.

The second takeaway is that in all of my selected BDC examples, we will be able to understand quite soon (already in Q1) what is the direction of travel for the underlying fundamentals.

The BDCs are down this year and trading at meaningful discounts to NAV (except for HTGC, which trades above NAV, but is down ~17% on a YTD and price return basis).

Now, as opposed to, say, FS KKR Capital (FSK) and Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN), which have registered a severe quality deterioration in their loan book (i.e., rising non-accruals and unrealized losses), none of the names that I have laid out here have experienced a material uptick in non-accruals.

For instance, such name as FDUS and HTGC have improving non-accrual statistics in Q4 compared to the prior quarter. In other words, I see no patterns, which would indicate serious troubles.

On top of that, all 5 BDCs except MSDL carry portfolio loan interest coverage levels at 2.5x or above (which is above average). In MSDL's case the interest coverage level stands at 2.3x, which is still relatively solid ration. There is a solid cushion built into these vehicles that should help withstand potential headwinds.

At the same time, as the table above reflects, all 5 BDCs will be soon tested on their PIK and SaaS loan underwritings. While the numbers are not huge in the context of their overall portfolios, it will be enough for us to make conclusions about the first patterns (i.e., in which direction PIK and SaaS loans go: fully repaid or amended, which would indicate unfavorable risks).

And the beauty here is that these 5 BDCs have a solid foundation, where I believe that the market will sooner or later relax the pressure on their P/NAV multiples, realizing that the credit losses will be nowhere close to those that are currently baked into the cake. Namely, there is no distorted picture with already shaky fundamentals like in the case of FSK and HRZN that would make it very difficult to spot new signals from risks that the market has recently priced in.

Summary

In my view, we all will be much smarter about the actual BDC situation when the Q1 2026 data emerge.

There will be definitely bears with actually valid arguments that the conventional metrics such as non-accruals and markdowns won't tell the full story now. And that would serve as another reason for keeping the discounts in place.

However, in such a situation when non-accruals and portfolio NAV land at stable levels (which is my base) and the bears continue to make the aforementioned argument, I will be looking under the hood and carefully assessing 2026 maturity loans.

If I don't see "amended and pretend" patterns, I will definitely be loading up more on BDCs.

data.title: AGQ: More Risk Than Reward Going Into Summer (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My last ProShares UltraPro Silver ETF (AGQ) article was a Buy suggestion in August 2023 here. Of course, back then nobody was interested in writing/reading about an approaching silver (XAGUSD:CUR) rally. However, this ETF's 2X leverage to a monster silver upmove since early 2024 has translated into spectacular returns for trust holders. AGQ's tremendous +311% price gain since that article handily crushed the equivalent period S&P 500 increase of +63%.

In fact, several years ago I was calling for silver prices as high as US$100 oz., when almost nobody believed $30 was possible, both authors and commenters on Seeking Alpha. (Silver reached an all-time peak of $120+ in January 2026.)

The bad news is the bull run is taking a breather now, and such could last another 3-6 months in my estimation. Too much excitement, too much hot money, too much of a good thing, I suppose.

Commodity markets rarely move in a straight line, and when they do, it's often intelligent to walk away. I have been suggesting such since late December here, and other gold/silver miner articles since the blow-off topping formation. I even sold off a large chunk of my physical silver (coins) around $86 an ounce in January.

2X Leverage Kills In A Bear Market

The problem with AGQ and any 2X leveraged-long ETF is bear markets in the underlying index or commodity prove quite fatal for investors. Typically, with extra costs to create the leverage, including decaying futures and forward contracts, ETF pricing can decline more than its leveraged-design equivalent to a 200% long exposure position. That's what has happened with AGQ since the January 28th silver peak.

The bad news for AGQ holders is time is not working on your side, like a traditional blue-chip equity investment on Wall Street. Not only do you have to contend with the immediate downside price risk of silver falling instead of rising, but the long-term costs to generate 2X leverage dramatically reduce returns.

Below I have drawn the rotten AGQ performance since January 28th's silver top. I am comparing total returns (including occasional dividends on cash holdings) to the 1X leverage found in the iShares Silver Trust (SLV). AGQ's -68.1% total return over roughly three months has been more than double SLV's -32.7% drop.

Next, I have drawn 5-year and 10-year charts comparing investment performance between the two. Notice the 1X design has bested AGQ over each span, all caused by extra operating expenses through time (alongside some daily compounding math issues). This makes silver market timing even more critical when putting AGQ in your portfolio.

Lastly, since 2009 AGQ has been an abysmal performer vs. SLV. Over 17 years, AGQ has only been able to climb by +159.6% in total value vs. SLV's +644.9% advance! To boot, the current AGQ $119 price stands far away from the split-adjusted all-time number above $720 in April 2011.

I highly suggest potential investors in AGQ read all of the disclaimers on the ETFs Fact Sheet here. Another important tax consideration is waiting for the K-1 form to be mailed/sent to you before the April 15th tax filing deadline. I have picked out some of the important ProShares information for review below.

Final Thoughts

I am projecting another large downdraw in silver is approaching, which could potentially lead to a longer-term bottom for bulls. A break below silver's 200-day moving average to a price under $60, perhaps as low as $50 seems to be the next major swing. I am hoping my 40 years of experience watching and trading precious metals through all kinds of macroeconomic and political circumstances guides me to the correct (although not mainstream) view of what's about to play out.

If this forecast is the future, I would expect AGQ to trade well under its 200-day moving average as well. My target price zone into late summer is $65 to $80, boxed in green below. In other words, if you were too emotional in your buy decision process or just new and unlucky to silver asset trading, buying AGQ at $425 in late January and riding it all the way to potentially $80 or less over less than six months, could be your unfortunate result for chasing a boom late in the rally. It should provide a life lesson on owning 2X leverage during a wicked commodity dump. I have been trying to persuade readers to avoid this situation for many months running.

How could my short-term bearishness on AGQ be proven the wrong investment choice? Fair question. If a new geopolitical reality supplants the Iran war news, silver could experience a heightened level of flight-to-safety money flows. Say, China invading Taiwan, or Russia attacking a NATO country may be required (in my opinion) to reverse the bearish silver setup.

More than likely, the scenario to drive silver lower will be a continuation of the crude oil spike causing worries about central bank tightening on unexpected inflation increases. The Iran war could easily encourage tighter conditions in the credit/bond markets globally regardless of U.S. Federal Reserve actions, maybe even a bear market in equities and recession into the summertime.

Silver pricing trends tend to follow: (1) industrial demand and (2) investor flows hedging worries about paper currency values. Coming off the most overbought condition for silver since early 1980, weaker industrial demand in a global recession on top of skittish investors/speculators selling silver to raise funds would be a double whammy difficult for silver's price to escape. The rising exchange margin requirements for futures trading since December have also been a major negative for price, like I have been explaining since the end of 2025.

Consequently, I feel an official downgraded rating for ProShares Ultra Silver to Sell from Buy seems to be in order. I would sell and wait patiently for lower pricing in AGQ before considering it a smart place to park money again.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: STMicroelectronics: Strong Q1 Confirms Cycle Turn And Upside Potential
data.description:
Our readers know that we have long covered STMicroelectronics (STM). In hindsight, it may seem straightforward to highlight, but this was a particularly strong call, and the pace of the share price appreciation (almost +80%) over such a short period (January 2026) exceeded our initial expectations (Fig. 1). As a reminder, the company did not provide an outlook for 2025, and we reiterated a "Neutral Stance Amid Earnings Pressure And Limited Visibility" in commenting on STM H1 2025 results.

Our recent buy rating was supported by a gradual improvement in underlying fundamentals and the prolonged share price weakness. The company has a strong balance sheet, and with underappreciated AI opportunities and moderated automotive pressures, there was a basis to regain operating leverage (and move our rating to Buy).

Fig 1

Q1 Results and Our Positive Stance

STM top-line sales reached $3.10 billion, with a year-on-year increase of 23% (Fig. 2). The company reported supportive results in the Microcontrollers segment, thanks to a rebound in Personal Electronics. Similarly, the Analog, MEMS, and Sensors segment grew a powerful +23.2%. The laggard remains the Power and Discrete (P&D) segment, with a 1.8% decline. That said, gross profit reached $1.05 billion, up 24.3% from last year. When gross profit margin increases at a higher rate than sales, it means the company is gaining back operating leverage. In the headline, STM reported negative FCF evolution; however, free cash flow included an $895 million cash outflow from the acquisition of NXP’s sensor segment.

Fig 2

Why are we still positive?

Firstly, STM reported its strongest earnings beat in almost three years. This reinforces our positive view that the cycle is turning. In our view, this marks the early phase of a multi-quarter upgrade cycle. This will be supported by cyclical recovery and additional upside from customer-driven demand, notably in AI data centers and low Earth orbit satellites (we are recently deep-diving into the sector with our cross-coverage in SES S.A.).

We also reported the positive STM's press release. They communicated that “in Q1, despite the macroeconomic uncertainty, we saw improving demand with strong booking and normalized inventory in distribution.” For Q2, the company is guiding top-line sales growth of around 12% quarter over quarter. Looking at the history, this is above seasonality and indicates a recovery story in place. STM top management is setting expectations for H2, anticipating a more normal seasonal pattern while still implying 19% year-on-year growth. Again, even if this comment is read as undermining, STM has faced allegations that it has not reported the correct outlook in the past. We view this positively. Given the inventory rebuild (due to a prolonged correction), we see a rebound in general-purpose MCUs as customers replenish their warehouses. This is also confirmed by a book-to-bill ratio well above 1. These signal healthy demand conditions.

There are also positive tailwinds ahead. Beyond the cyclical recovery, several company-specific drivers are likely to contribute to earnings growth. For the first time, we have evidence to report. We see incremental top-line sales from key customers such as Apple and Tesla. That said, AI data centers and LEO satellites will combine to reach $500 billion and $1 billion in 2026 and 2027, respectively (Fig. 3). Even if we cannot price this development, the new Apple CEO might provide a tailwind for STM. In the past, he has overseen hardware across Apple's product portfolio, and we might expect new hardware features in each iPhone. Being STM's leading supplier for Cupertino, this is a plus to report.

Fig 3

On profitability, the Q2 gross margin came in ahead of Wall Street expectations. This was supported by 1) a more favorable product mix and 2) lower underutilization costs. The company sees further improvement in the year.

Lastly, STM announced a multi-year partnership with AWS. This multi-year, dollar commercial engagement will support the company's infrastructure and position STM as a critical supplier for the AI data center buildout. Key to the report is the NXP MEMS Sensor Acquisition. This was dated in early February and is an asset that STM has been looking to buy for a long time. This strategic acquisition will support the product portfolio in automotive safety and industrial sensors.

Earnings Changes and Valuation

There are many moving parts in assessing the STM 2026 financial forecast. On the top line, we increased our sales to €14.5 billion (from €13.6 billion), implying a sales pickup of almost 23% versus 2025 numbers. Regarding gross margin evolution, the company reported improved capacity absorption and cost discipline. Here at the Lab, we were already anticipating a higher gross margin at 35.7%. This is still aligned with STM's estimates. This results in an EBIT margin of 11% at €1.59 billion. Despite the acquisition, the company has a positive net cash position (Fig. 4), so we slightly increase profit before tax by €50 million, bringing it to €1.64 billion. Applying the STM tax rate (16%), we increase our net profit to €1.37 billion. In our calculation, we used a 16% tax rate, and considering the 911 million outstanding shares, STM's 2026 EPS is set at €1.51. Naturally, if we apply our P/E target of 20x, we should move STM to sell with a share price of €30. However, the company is in a recovery mood, with a 2027 EPS of €2.

At this stage, and somewhat departing from our usual fundamentals-based approach, we apply a 25x P/E multiple, in line with peers, to the average 2027 EPS estimates (which we believe will be revised upward over time). This results in a target price of €50 per share ($58.5 in ADR).

Fig 4

Risks

STM downside risks include continued underperformance in the Power & Discrete segment and higher-than-expected unused industrial capacity. We still believe the Automotive market will continue to suffer (and that Infineon is in a better position than STM in this segment, also thanks to a stronger product offering in China). Slower innovation and additional CAPEX are downside risks to consider. We positively report the latest inventory trends, but this remains an area of monitoring. A weakening dollar might not be playing any favor to STM earnings. Being a semis producer, STM needs to constantly improve its product portfolio, so any product failure has a multi-year commercial headwind. Compared to our last update, this analysis identifies multiple valuation risks that are already priced in, with an earnings recovery ahead.

Conclusion

STMicroelectronics has delivered an exceptionally strong start to the year. The Q1 performance shows a dramatic acceleration in sales. The latest acquisition and AWS partnerships support the company. We remain buyers.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dell's AI Moment: Why Inference And CPUs (Not GPUs), Is Driving The Next Growth Cycle
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since my last coverage of Dell Technologies (DELL), in which I remained bullish based on the company’s strong ISG growth and robust AI infrastructure demand despite the margin pressure from rising DRAM/NAND memory prices, the stock jumped over 60%.

In this article, I examine how Bank of America’s (BoA) report regarding raised sales for the server CPU industry due to inference from AI agentic models benefits DELL and could lead to a new growth cycle.

About risks, almost the same risks as in my previous article continue to exist, namely low profit margins, price pressure, and high competition in the CSG segment, as well as supply chain limitations.

ISG Segment Continues to Lead Dell’s Growth Story

The big picture is that the company reported 19% YoY growth in FY26, along with 31% operating income growth. Specifically, the AI-related Infrastructure Solutions Group (ISG) segment grew by 40% in FY26—with the AI-Optimized Servers Revenue business line increasing by 166% and the Traditional Servers & Networking Revenue business just 9%—while the PC-related segment, Client Solutions Group (CSG), grew only 5%, and operating income declined by 5%.

Also, the company disclosed $43B in AI backlog, with $34.1B booked in Q4’26, while the company stated that “for FY '27, (we) expect $50 billion in AI revenue, about 100% growth year-over-year,” reflecting that DELL continues to have strong momentum in the AI business.

The company runs under two completely different operating models, in terms of revenue and profit.

The ISG segment is DELL’s fastest-growing segment in terms of revenue; notably, only in Q4’26 it grew 73% YoY; however, due to its capital‑intensive nature, it has a low operating margin of about 11.7% in FY26, down compared to about 12.8% in the previous year. Similarly, the CSG segment holds an operating margin of 5.5% in FY26, compared to 6.14% in the previous year, but, as already mentioned, remains relatively flat on a full‑year basis, despite the 14% revenue growth in Q4. The decrease in CSG’s operating margin was a result of the company’s strategy not to increase prices in order to gain market share, despite the increasing prices of DRAM (5.5x increase in six months) and NAND (4x increase in six months) memory. That said, Dell’s business grew 18%, while the market grew 10%, meaning that Dell gained 100 basis points of share.

In contrast, specific business lines within both segments increase the company’s overall profitability, which means Dell’s profitability is highly sensitive to sales mix. Dell‑IP storage solutions and OpEx discipline, which was down 320 bps to 9.9% of revenue, boost the company’s profitability. If you want to learn more about the Dell‑IP business, refer to my previous coverage.

Investors need to know that, despite the massive demand for AI servers, the company does not anticipate any margin expansion in that segment. Especially, it is positioned as: “Profitability is in line with our mid-single-digit operating margin target. We like our position, the line of sight we have with our backlog and pipeline”.

Given that, as investors, Dell’s profitability remains a risk concern. OpEx reductions and the Dell‑IP business contribute to profitability, but both have limitations regarding how much they could contribute in the long term. I would like to see AI server products and the CSG segment achieve higher operating margins due to pricing power and provide a sustainable profitable business model, rather than OpEx and sales mix being the main margin drivers.

Overall, the company achieved net income growth of 39% YoY, along with a 5.23% profit margin in FY26, compared to about 4.79% in FY25

Inference Creates a New Cycle of Growth, CPUs Will Be the “Star”, and Not GPUs

Let me start with the company’s management statement:

Moving to traditional servers… We saw broad-based strength with units up, a larger active buyer base and a richer mix of our 16th and 17th generation platforms as customers shifted to dense high-performance configurations. The ROI to refresh is compelling. Even at higher ASPs, customers see a 7:1 consolidation when upgrading from the 14th generation to our latest platforms… Traditional x86 is benefiting from AI infrastructure build-outs. While many AI workloads rely on specialized GPUs, traditional compute remains essential for orchestration, data processing and inference support.

Combining the above statement with BoA’s report, which says:

We believe CPUs are increasingly becoming essential in sequential and latency-sensitive workloads, and are an integral part of overall AI infrastructure. The team expects CPUs to consistently make up an approximately 5% share of the overall AI data center total addressable market (TAM) of approximately $1.4 trillion. They estimate a +21% compound annual growth rate (CAGR) toward a TAM of $70+ billion by 2030, up from approximately $28 billion in 2025.

Now, take into account that Morgan Stanley said, also said that agentic AI systems could increase CPU demand by 2030, changing data center buildouts and spreading AI spending beyond GPUs

What do all the above mean?

In a single phrase, in my view, means a new growth cycle for the company!

Let me explain. The inference of AI agents and tools -not just chatbots like ChatGPT or Gemini- requires more CPU per GPU due to CPUs are responsible for managing orchestration and data handling. In simpler terms, CPUs are responsible for organizing the various steps that an AI agent needs to follow in order to provide an answer.

For this reason, today, as of April 24, 2026, Intel says that AI inference pushes the GPU/CPU ratio to change from 1:8 toward 1:1. For DELL, this means a new cycle of demand for new server configurations, especially designed for a 1:1 ratio. Remember that Dell does not sell CPUs but sells servers designed to include GPUs, CPUs, memory, cooling systems, and other staff—this is called rack systems. Moreover, the company stated that clients replace their older servers with the new 16th and 17th generation platforms, even if they are at higher prices.

Dell buys CPUs and GPUs from other companies, such as Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Intel (INTC), and could combine them with its branded memory, cooling systems, and software, and offer a full rack system to AI infrastructure companies.

Given all the above, I believe that the company has entered a new cycle of growth!

Valuation and Conclusion

Overall, the company seems undervalued compared to the sector, despite its YTD price return of 71%, reaching a record high price.

In terms of growth, the company is expected to achieve double‑digit revenue growth in the following years. In my view, Dell could achieve this growth rate for a longer period, given that the estimation below is prior to BoA’s and Morgan Stanley’s analysis. Therefore, a FWD P/S below 1 is more than attractive.

In terms of FWD P/E, Dell also has attractive metrics, given that it is entering a new growth cycle.

My main concern remains the company’s traditional segment, the CSG, which was almost flat in FY’26 and whose operating income decreased by 5% YoY. Overall, the segment seems weak, given that it is a highly cyclical segment and currently is in a down phase of the cycle.

Given the successful track record and the massive backlog, I am not concerned about an AI slowdown that could reduce the company’s growth trajectory, but I do see supply chain limitations and fluctuations as risks. The company stated:

Across the industry, the environment remains highly dynamic with unprecedented AI demand creating sustained supply tightness and frequent pricing resets.

This means that Dell may need to face even higher NAND/DRAM memory prices. Given that it has long-term agreements (LTAs) and capacity agreements with its suppliers, it helps to minimize supply chain risk, but it is still a key aspect to monitor. Also, Dell seems to have pricing power with price adjustments, as in servers it changed prices in mid-December, aiming to stabilize margins due to the higher memory costs.

In conclusion, Dell remains an undervalued stock, in my view, with impressive revenue growth and room for further expansion. Inference via AI agents could boost the company’s server business, but investors need to monitor its profitability and supply chain limitations.

Given all the above, I rerate the stock as BUY.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks Still Has Room To Run Despite A Rich Valuation
data.description:
Palo Alto Networks (PANW) still looks strong heading into Q3 and beyond. The company keeps posting big numbers in Next-Generation Security (NGS), has made good moves with deals like CyberArk and Chronosphere, and keeps growing both recurring revenue and profit margins, with Q2 NGS ARR at $6.33 billion, up 33% year-over-year, and non-GAAP operating margin at 30.3%. I’m giving it a Buy rating here. The growth story doesn’t look shaky, and the upside seems worth it, even if the stock isn’t cheap. Expectations are already high, but management keeps hitting targets and signaling more gains ahead, so I think that’s fair.

Shares go for about 47 times expected earnings and about 12.5 times next year’s sales. Those are steep numbers for a cybersecurity company, but not totally out of line given how fast ARR is growing, how much cash the business throws off, and how much market share they keep taking. The company’s margins are also heading up, and there’s a clear path for that to continue. What stands out is that PANW keeps delivering both on sales and profits, and they’re actually making progress on those longer-term goals they keep talking about.

NGS Keeps Expanding

The NGS platform now covers more ground with CyberArk and Chronosphere on board. For Q3, they’re targeting $7.94 to $7.96 billion in NGS ARR, up 56% from last year. About $1.47 billion of that is coming from the new acquisitions, which is a big chunk, but it doesn’t take away from the fact that their NGS business was already growing fast. The numbers back that up: last quarter, NGS ARR hit $6.33 billion, up 33% year-over-year, including a $200 million contribution from Chronosphere; on an organic basis, NGS ARR was up 28% year-over-year. CyberArk is a big deal for them; identity security is getting more important, and as of December 2025, the CyberArk platform was pulling in over $1.2 billion in identity-related NGS ARR, up 30% from the year before. Management says, “Our Q3 and full year 2026 guidance assumes reported NGS ARR for CyberArk will be approximately 2% to 3% lower than the equivalent on the CyberArk previous bookings-based ARR definition,” so they’re being conservative about how fast they’ll actually convert those bookings into recurring revenue, which should help avoid any ugly surprises as they finish integrating everything.

Margins Keep Climbing

PANW’s main growth drivers are showing up in profits. In Q2, the company hit a 30.3% non-GAAP operating margin, up 190 basis points from last year. Over the past twelve months, free cash flow came in at $3.75 billion, with a margin of 37.9%. Management says they still want to reach a 40% free cash flow margin by FY28 and $20 billion in NGS ARR by 2030. They’re not just saying that for show; they keep tracking progress quarter by quarter, trying to make the most of integration and getting more out of headcount. From management: “Integration is being executed with the same rigor that we apply to running our core business… [with] clear governance… and measures to ensure continuity for customers, partners, and employees.” In other words, they’re keeping things steady. For Q3, they’re projecting revenue between $2.941 and $2.945 billion, up 28--29% year-over-year. Non-GAAP EPS is expected at $0.78 to $0.80. For the full year, they see revenue landing between $11.28 and $11.31 billion (22-23% up) and EPS at $3.65-$3.70. They expect to keep an operating margin of around 28.5-29% through FY26 and hold the free cash flow margin at 37%.

Acquisitions Add Pressure

Management isn’t pretending all this growth comes easy. Expanding the product lineup this fast, while facing about $4.9 billion in combined cash outlay across Chronosphere and CyberArk, with CyberArk closing after Q2 ended, brings real challenges. There’s a short-term hit to EPS from all the M&A. They acknowledge that but say the “focus is on a frictionless onboarding experience… to unlock the full value of these acquisitions.” The SASE platform is one of the clearest signs things are working; it’s now over $1.5 billion in ARR, up about 40% from last year. Cortex XSIAM crossed the $0.5 billion ARR mark, with more than 600 customers spending almost $1 million each in ARR. Both have pulled in new customers and managed to get more business from the existing base.

Cash Still Supports

The balance sheet looks strong. Free cash flow holds up, both in dollar terms and as a percent of revenue, which lets them keep buying back shares, including the new $1 billion authorization they announced in March 2026. At the end of Q2, they had $4.158 billion in cash and equivalents, or $7.898 billion including investments, and that figure already included the $2.6 billion cash outlay for Chronosphere. There’s another $2.3 billion earmarked for the CyberArk deal in Q3, but even after that, there’s still plenty of room to spend more if they want to move on to other deals or just stay flexible, assuming they don’t get sloppy with discipline.

Big picture, the industry’s still got a lot going for it. Companies keep moving toward bundled security platforms, AI-powered threat detection, and cloud-first tools. All of these trends look like they’ll keep boosting demand for several years. The company’s platform pitch is landing. Growth in SASE and AI-driven SOC products shows up in sales cycles. Analyst coverage mirrors this, with Benchmark initiating at Buy, Wells Fargo maintaining Overweight, and Citi calling the post-earnings reaction nothing but a “short-term speed bump.” There’s also a premium building in the sector for companies with a shot at leading in AI and quantum security. Both analysts and the company itself keep pointing to new launches, Prisma AIRS, a secure browser, and quantum-ready certificate management as proof that interest and adoption aren’t fading.

Risks

Pulling off integrations at this size isn’t simple. If they mess up with either product or sales alignment for CyberArk or Chronosphere, that could break their growth path and force investors to rethink the stock. They depend on big distributors; three of them handle 42% of all revenue and nearly half of what’s owed to them as of January 2026. If any of those relationships weaken, or if incentives get out of sync, they could face real trouble. Price competition is always lurking. The company even says, “The sales prices of our products, subscriptions, and support offerings may decline for a variety of reasons, including competitive pricing pressures…” That would hit their margins if they can’t keep innovating or cutting costs. Broader economic stuff matters too. If overseas business hits snags, especially in a bad economy or if politics get tense, then working capital slows down, and short-term cash flow and operating results can come under pressure.

Valuation Needs Execution

The stock trades at a pretty steep premium. Still, the price doesn’t assume everything will go perfectly. If they hit the top end of their goals for fiscal 2026, $11.31 billion in revenue, $3.70 EPS, and can push free cash flow margins to at least 40% by FY28, then the stock should be able to hold onto its current earnings multiple, maybe even see it go higher, as long as NGS ARR and overall margins don’t suddenly slow down.

Next, I’m watching a few things to see if the story holds up: NGS ARR each quarter (the target for FY26 is $8.52-$8.62 billion, which includes $1.52 billion from M&A), operating margins moving toward the FY26 range, free cash flow margin moving toward the FY28 goal, and whether SASE, Cortex, and Identity segments keep growing as they bring everything together. How well they bring in and grow the CyberArk and Chronosphere businesses will show up in retention, cross-sell, and ARR; they’ll need to prove those deals are working. I think the combined platform can still bring in more ARR as PANW pushes Prisma AIRS, the secure browser, and quantum-ready certificate management. That’s backed up by what they’re saying on recent earnings calls.

Bull Case Holds

Big picture, PANW is combining strong growth, profitability, and product breadth, with Q2 NGS ARR up 33% year-over-year and non-GAAP operating margin at 30.3%. The bull case doesn’t need any crazy assumptions, just solid execution, pulling together all the pieces, and taking more market share, which seems to be happening. The path to $20 billion NGS ARR by 2030 and 40% free cash flow margins by FY28 isn’t easy, but with every quarter, it looks more doable. Most of the risk is still skewed to the upside, and that’s supported by both the fundamentals and what’s happening in the sector. If they start having real trouble pulling everything together, margins keep shrinking, or NGS ARR falls short, I’ll change my mind. For now, the odds look pretty good for the bulls.

data.title: PHK: NAV Trending Slightly Higher But Still Not A Buy
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the PIMCO High Income Fund (PHK), I issued a hold rating due to the flat earnings and questionable dividend coverage at the time.
