Since then, the fund's share price has declined following continued headwinds in the debt markets. High interest rates continue to put pressure on credit funds and increase their risk profile. Since my last coverage, PHK has released an updated semi-annual report that I wanted to use to reassess the fund's earnings, performance, and valuation.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that PHK's share price has only slightly recovered from the initial decline related to the deterioration of the debt markets. Meanwhile, PHK's total return jumps up to 12.6% over the same time frame. PHK now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 12.3% while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. Dividend coverage can certainly improve going forward, but I anticipate that PHK is likely to keep payouts consistent for the remainder of the year.

Unfortunately, PHK's success seems to be reliant on a lower interest rate environment. Although the NAV has steadily climbed higher over the last few years, I don't expect there to be any meaningful NAV growth until interest rates are lowered. A lower cost of debt would encourage more borrowing activity and higher valuations for PHK's portfolio. The challenge is that we don't know how much longer interest rates will remain elevated. Until interest rates are lowered, the dividend coverage is likely to also remain thin, which can threaten the appeal for income investors seeking safety from the equity markets.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, PHK has total managed assets of $1.1B that are spread across a diverse range of income-producing securities. The fund's primary goal is to generate a high level of income, so the success of PHK is not correlated to the movement of the equity markets. Instead, PHK's success is reliant on borrowers' ability to keep up with their required debt maintenance over time. In order to get the high yield it needs to support distributions, PHK allocates portions of its portfolio to low-rated securities and some global positions. For instance, the breakdown by region is as follows:

  • United States: 63.33%
  • Bermuda: 3.41%
  • France: 3.30%
  • United Kingdom: 3.02%
  • Colombia: 2.74%

Additionally, the fund also maintains diversity across different industries. PHK is most heavily allocated to technology investments at 7.83% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to healthcare and wireless investments, which accounts for 4.41% and 3.93% of assets, respectively. However, I think it's worth mentioning that PHK's larger allocation to technology securities may be a source of risk during a time period where the markets continue to punish software companies. While there's limited visibility into how much of PHK's technology investments are specifically exposed to software, it is still something to consider if you believe AI will continue to drive the software sector lower.

PHK also tends to lean more heavily on short-dated securities that have maturities of less than five years out. Shorter-dated securities tend to offer more resilience to fluctuations in interest rates, but it also means that management has to actively work harder to replace these securities once they reach maturity. Under a high interest rate environment, replacing these short-dated securities can be difficult. Conversely, longer-dated maturities tend to be more sensitive to high interest rates but can offer a secure source of income within the portfolio for a longer period of time.

Updated Performance Metrics

When I previously covered PHK, the fund traded at a premium to NAV of 6.22%. Following the recent decline in share price, PHK now trades at a slightly lower premium to NAV of 2.64%. While this may not seem like the most attractive valuation, we have to consider that the price still trades below PHK's typical range. Referring to the red line below, we can see that this is actually near the lower end of PHK's historical price-to-NAV range. For instance, the fund has traded at an average premium to NAV of 6.5% over the last five years.

Since my last coverage, the fund has also released an updated semi-annual report that provides us with some transparency into its earnings. Net investment income amounted to $0.24 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.09 per share. Assuming that PHK is capable of achieving similar results through the second half of this period, the fund is on track to generate net investment income that falls within its typical historical range. This is a great indication that the underlying portfolio strategy is efficient at generating income through changing market conditions.

However, I dislike the fact that PHK's net investment income doesn't support the distribution in its entirety. While this isn't necessarily a big deal, it does introduce a vulnerability to market downturns. Since PHK does not generate sufficient net investment income by itself, the fund has to depend on net realized gains to bridge any gaps in earnings. This is a common dynamic for high-yield closed-end funds, but it does mean that it puts PHK at the risk of paying out more than it earns.

If a fund is frequently paying out more than it earns, the underlying NAV will erode over time. This is usually only a big deal during periods of market declines. A good example of this was demonstrated through 2022's market decline, which resulted in PHK reporting large net realized losses of $1.14 per share. This large net realized loss resulted in PHK's NAV declining, which the fund still hasn't fully recovered from. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $5.01 per share
  • 2021: $5.92 per share
  • 2022: $4.72 per share
  • 2023: $4.51 per share
  • 2024: $4.56 per share
  • 2025: $4.62 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $4.67 per share

As we can see, PHK's NAV has failed to maintain enough positive momentum to get back to its high of 2021. On the bright side, PHK has been able to steadily increase its NAV over the last few years. As long as we don't see any further headwinds in the debt markets, such as an increase in interest rates, PHK's growth may continue.

Outlook Risks

Despite the fund's short-term improvement of its NAV growth, the long-term performance makes it clear that there are some structural flaws that need to be improved. For instance, PHK's share price started its decline long before the volatility of the pandemic. Over the last ten years, PHK's share price declined by roughly 49.7%, despite market indices racing to all-time highs over the last decade. When including all distributions paid out to investors, the total return jumps up to 53.5% over the same time frame. Although the total return is positive, I don't believe it justifies the erosion of capital.

However, the main contributor to PHK's suppressed share price since 2022 is the high interest rate environment. Since PHK's positions tend to share an inverse relation with interest rates, the value of its holdings declined as soon as the Fed hiked interest rates to new highs over the last ten years. During the latest Fed meeting, it was decided to leave interest rates unchanged. I believe there are two ways that high interest rates will continue to be a headwind for PHK:

  1. Limited growth opportunities for management to invest in.
  2. Elevated probability of borrower defaults will keep the market pessimistic.

Since PHK leans most heavily on high-yield credit investments, underlying earnings growth relies on management's ability to allocate capital towards new borrowers. However, the simple truth is that a higher cost of debt limits the number of borrowers in the market seeking debt financing. A high cost of debt causes borrowers to wait until interest rates fall so that their interest expenses will be less of a burden.

The second risk going forward would be the exposure to borrower defaults. Even if PHK's portfolio doesn't specifically go through any defaults, PHK's share price can be negatively impacted by the overall sentiment. After all, PHK does have some exposure to securities with poor credit ratings. For reference, securities with a credit rating below a BBB tend to be riskier. These are borrowers that are more reliant on debt to support operations or have less consistent cash flows. The fund's breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 58.89%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 34.14%
  • Not Rated: 6.98%

Looking forward, I believe the greatest catalyst for PHK would be lower interest rates. A lower cost of debt would create a more favorable borrowing environment and may cause an influx of new opportunities in the market. Furthermore, the value of its positions will increase, and PHK's spread between the cost of its debt and the yield collected from securities will improve.

Dividend

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.048 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 12.3%. Based on the fund's payout history over the last few years, it's safe to assume that PHK will likely keep payouts unchanged for the remainder of the year. This would result in PHK issuing an annual payout totaling $0.576 per share. Unfortunately, the fund has a very mixed history for income investors. Once again, payouts were already trending lower before the volatile market conditions caused by the pandemic.

I would actually argue that a dividend cut would be beneficial for the fund's long-term success. With a starting yield of 12.3%, PHK can implement a 20% dividend reduction. This would leave PHK with a starting dividend yield somewhere around 9.8%, which is still highly competitive among the high yield landscape. A lower payout would also improve the fund's spread by reducing the pressure of generating a high level of income. A lower payout can lead to excess cash flow for PHK, which could then be reinvested back into new growth opportunities and improve future earnings.

However, I believe that payouts should be able to be sustained going forward when referring to the latest UNII report. Based on the report, which is timed from March 2025 to March 2026, we can see that PHK has a dividend coverage rate of 92.3%. Although the fund doesn't completely support all of its distributions with earnings, PKH is actually one of the funds with a stronger distribution coverage rate above the 90% market. I attribute any gaps in coverage to the unfavorable market environment of higher interest rates, which is limited growth potential.

Lastly, investors should be aware that distributions received from PHK may not offer tax-efficiency. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 81.49% of the distributions paid YTD were classified as net investment income, which has the highest tax consequences for investors. Therefore, I believe that PHK is best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that PHK is still a hold at this time. Despite the fund's price-to-NAV valuation becoming a bit more attractive, I believe that PHK's growth potential is quite limited as long as interest rates remain elevated. The NAV has experienced a slow uptick over the last few years, but the portfolio structure reveals that PHK is reliant on net realized gains to drive growth over time. So until interest rates are substantially lowered, I don't believe that PHK offers a meaningful value proposition at this time. Furthermore, the dividend coverage remains tight, so if defaults occur, net investment income is likely to fall and weaken payouts.
